data.title: Columbia Sportswear Tries To Restart Growth In A Competitive Market
data.description:
Columbia Sportswear (COLM) is set to report its Q4 2025 earnings on February 3. The final quarter of the year is always a crucial one for the company as it is its largest revenue producer. This year’s is perhaps even more meaningful because it should provide greater visibility for the effect of Columbia Sportswear's new ACCELERATE growth strategy that is designed to attract younger consumers and elevate the image of its brands.

The outdoor and athletic apparel company faces fierce competition from almost every angle. VF Corporation (VFC) produces high-quality apparel and shoes with its North Face and Timberland brands. Under Armour (UAA) is fighting to regain its relevance in performance apparel. Privately held Patagonia has strong brand recognition and commands a premium price for its outdoor wear. Even big-name brands like Nike (NKE) and Lululemon (LULU) have some crossover into the spaces where Columbia is trying to carve out a bigger market share.

To its credit, Columbia does have good brand recognition and recently announced a “brand refresh" that aims to differentiate its name from the pack of competitors. A new ad campaign launched in the US last August with a rollout in other parts of the world to follow. The upcoming earnings report may give a small glimpse of what those marketing efforts will bring, but as of yet, it is too soon to tell if COLM can climb back to the profitability it enjoyed a few years ago. For now, I see the stock has a Hold for the reasons discussed below.

Company Overview

Columbia Sportswear Company describes itself as a global leader in the production and sales of outdoor and athletic apparel, footwear, and accessories. While the company produces most of its goods under its namesake brand, Columbia Sportswear also operates under the brand names Sorel, Mountain Hardwear, and prAna. Sorel is a producer of premium footwear that was acquired by COLM in 2000. Mountain Hardwear designs and sells gear for mountain climbing and outdoor activities. The final subsidiary, prAna, creates outerwear for climbing and yoga. As shown in the chart below, a large majority of the company’s sales from 2024 came from the Columbia brand in the US.

Columbia was founded in the 1930s as Columbia Hat Company. It became a publicly traded company in 1998. Today, Columbia Sportswear is second only to Nike (NKE) in the state of Oregon for athletic and outdoor apparel sales. The company employs almost 10,000 workers and reported $3.37 billion in sales in 2024. In FY 2025, the company is expected to fall just short of that mark at $3.36 billion in sales. If COLM does not surpass last year’s top line number, it will mark the third straight year of declining revenues for the company.

As with its revenues, its bottom line has been on an extended decline as well. Normalized earnings per share of $4.31 in FY 2022 fell to $3.58 in FY 2023 and $3.19 in FY 2024. Barring an upside surprise in Q4 2025, the company’s bottom line will shrink again in 2025.

COLM does pay a dividend that is close to the average for its sector. The quarterly payout of $0.30 has been consistent since Q1 2022. Some analysts expect the company to cut its distribution in the next two years even though its current payout ratio of 39% is only slightly elevated from its peers.

Balance Sheet Check Up

At the end of Q3 2025, Columbia Sportswear reported total assets of $2.7 billion, 8.8% less than at the end of 2024 but only 1.7% less than Q3 2024. The largest line item for its recent asset total belonged to inventories at just over $800 million. The company also reported accounts receivable of $610 million and cash of $229 million. Perhaps most interesting about the company's balance sheet is the absence of any real long-term debt.

Quarter-by-quarter comparisons for the company are challenging because so much of Columbia Sportswear’s business is seasonable. The company brings in its greatest share of revenue in the fourth quarter as demand for winter apparel increases along with a boost from the end-of-year holidays. When Q3 2025 is compared to Q3 2024, inventories were basically flat while the company’s cash on hand had decreased by just over 25%.

One additional factor in the decreasing assets reported by the company was related to goodwill. Earlier in 2025, Columbia Sportswear recorded goodwill impairment for both its Mountain Hardwear and prAna brands. As of Q3, COLM had just $5.7 million in goodwill on its balance sheet compared to $26.7 at the end of 2024.

Stockholders’ equity has been on a downward trend since the end of 2021, when it stood at $1.99 billion. It dropped to $1.78 billion by the end of 2024. As of September 30, 2025, it was only $1.66 billion.

One positive working in favor of COLM is its reduction in outstanding shares that has been taking place consistently for almost a decade. In its final SEC filing for 2022, the company reported 64.5 million outstanding shares. As part of the company’s 10-K for 2024, there were 59.8 million shares outstanding. As of October 24, 2025, Columbia Sportswear reported 53.9 million common shares. The company still has $455.9 million remaining on a previous authorization to repurchase its stock. That total represents 16% of the company's current $2.85 billion market cap.

Analysis of COLM

In the first nine months of 2024, sales in the US, where the company generates more revenue than all other world markets combined, declined by 2.4%. However, other regions showed improvement. In EMEA (Europe, Middle East, and Asia), sales were up 14.9%. In LAAP (Latin American and Asia Pacific), sales increased 9.3%. Canada managed to eke out an improvement of 0.6%. All totaled, sales were up 2.4% in the first three quarters of the year. Further details from the company’s most recent earnings presentation can be seen below, with a breakdown of the performance from each of the company’s four brands.

Unfortunately, during this same time period, not all of the sales increases were able to filter down to the bottom line. For EMEA and LAAP, operating income rose by 18.0% and 12.7%, respectively. However, in the US, operating income declined by 14.9%. In Canada, operating income dropped 11.0%. Total operating income decreased 32.3% after unallocated corporate expenses were factored in as the operating margin decreased by 200 basis points, falling to 3.9%.

Cash flow is an issue for the company, as its cash position in the first nine months of 2025 decreased 25.4% when compared to the same time period in 2024. This drop occurred even though the company spent less on buybacks and dividends. The biggest difference for the company was a $130 change in accounts payable that was caused by a buildup in inventory that was much larger than what was shown in the previous year.

Much of the hope for the future of Columbia Sportswear relies on its profit improvement program known as ACCELERATE. The strategy focuses on both cost reductions as well as revenue improvement. For cost savings, the company is planning cuts in operations and improvements in indirect, or non-inventory, spending. In 2024, the company stated that these actions saved roughly $90 million. When those savings are added to the progress made in the first nine months of last year, the company has identified cost reductions that exceed $150 million. Future plans will focus on selling, general, and administrative expenses to help boost operating margin over time.

The ACCELERATE strategy is designed to spur future growth while also strengthening the core elements of the company. The company aims to attract younger, more active consumers while also servicing existing consumers with its traditional offerings. A fully integrated marketing campaign that uses both “old school” channels as well as social media is designed to create higher and more efficient demand to boost sales.

Q4 Earnings Expectations

Columbia Sportswear is set to release its Q4 and FY 2025 financial results on February 3. Analysts expect the company to report GAAP earnings of $1.21 per share. Even if COLM is able to report a mild surprise to the upside, Q4 2025 will likely mark the third straight year of declining profits in the final three months of the year. Below is a chart that shows the progress, or lack thereof, that Columbia Sportswear has made since 2021.

GAAP EPS

Increase/Decrease

Q4 2021

$2.39

-

Q4 2022

$2.47

+ 3.3%

Q4 2023

$1.87

- 24.3%

Q4 2024

$1.80

- 3.7%

Q4 2025 (Est.)

$1.21

- 32.8%

Based on data available for retail sales at the end of 2025, I think investors have reason to be cautiously optimistic for COLM. Traffic in retail stores was up 3% on Black Friday, and online sales surged 7.7% in the post-Thanksgiving period. Both Black Friday and Cyber Monday had record spending, which is good for companies like Columbia that are pushing more of a direct-to-consumer model. However, some of the boost in sales may have come from “buy now, pay later” transactions that could hamper sales in future quarters.

Holiday sales at privately held REI appeared to be elevated by a number of sales and promotions. However, expectations for Dick’s Sporting Goods (DKS) are less promising. In the last 90 days, DKS has seen its Q4 2025 earnings estimates be revised downward 17 times, compared to only two improvements.

Investors may have to wait until Q1 or Q2 2026 to see the real impact of the company’s recent marketing and design efforts. Columbia Sportswear rolled out a new, limited edition line of clothing on January 16, showing an additional push to reach a younger audience. The company may also draw some added interest from its sponsorship of the US Curling team at the 2026 Winter Olympics.

Additional Risks to Consider

The value of the Columbia Sportswear brand has been under pressure for an extended period of time. To help boost its brand image, the company is investing in marketing efforts and opening what it calls a “small number” of premium stores in high-traffic areas in North America. Changing the perception of consumers is a slow process, and this is not one that has a guarantee of success. The company also runs the risk of alienating more value-conscious buyers with the shift to a more premium product.

The apparel and accessories business is highly competitive. Columbia Sportswear has extensive competition both in the US and overseas. In order to maintain sales levels, the company may have to cut prices or offer other incentives that could impact profit margins. Consumer tastes and preferences for various goods can also change quickly. This makes adequate maintenance of production and inventory levels especially challenging. The development of new products and the lead time needed to bring them to market could make it difficult for the company to respond to new trends in the marketplace.

Tariffs have been an issue for almost all apparel and footwear companies over the last year. As part of Columbia Sportswear’s last earnings call, the company stated that it was using a number of different tactics to mitigate the impact of higher tariffs. These include reducing production, negotiating better deals with vendors, and price increases, which may not be well received by consumers. Tariffs and other trade issues can also impact supply chains and the timing for products to arrive to market.

Even though Columbia Sportswear went public 28 years ago, it is still largely controlled by one family. As part of its quarterly filings with the SEC, COLM stated that three related shareholders, including new co-president Joseph P. Boyle, control just over 50% of the outstanding common stock. As such, this gives the family members “significant influence over all matters requiring shareholder approval.” The company also stated that the shares owned by these family members could be liquidated, in part, if those individuals needed to pay “estate or unrealized gains tax obligations.”

The Bottom Line

Even though the stock for Columbia Sportswear has declined by over 40% in the last 12 months, it is still not trading at a significant bargain to its sector peers. There are no guarantees that its current strategy to improve its brand image will have a meaningful positive impact on its bottom line. The nine analysts that cover the company are not optimistic either, as they have an average earnings per share estimate in FY 2026 that is less than expectations for FY 2025. Without greater visibility on just what lies ahead, COLM is a Hold.

data.title: Invesco Mortgage Capital: Double-Digit Yield Only Makes Sense If You Watch Book Value
data.description:
Introduction

Invesco Mortgage Capital Inc. (IVR) has an aggressively high yield, but both its recent dividend history and its long term-returns are disappointing. A closer look shows high payout ratios, poor dividend growth, and relatively high leverage. Such poor metrics would get flagged on any normal dividend screen, notwithstanding the high yield.

However, this mortgage REIT has been going through a portfolio transition that cannot be ignored. Almost its entire $5.7 billion portfolio today comprises Agency RMBS and Agency CMBS, with very little non-agency exposure. The portfolio is funded primarily through repo borrowings. The portfolio now has a 6.7-6.9x leverage range, and a weighted average yield of ~5.4%. IVR is no longer a credit instrument; rather, it is a levered agency mortgage REIT with returns coming from rate volatility and funding spreads.

This change is already reflected in its metrics. IVR is now trading roughly at book value after a 6-10% hike in BV since Q3. In Q3 itself, book value was up 4.5%, offering an economic return of ~8.7% when combined with dividends. This is an important difference from most mREITs - many of which tend to gradually erode book value, especially during volatile periods. That IVR was able to both grow book value while paying a high dividend is a positive surprise.

The dividend - at $0.12 per month (~$1.44 annualized, ~15–16% yield) - is indeed high. However, management has clearly told investors that payout now roughly matches the portfolio’s levered net return on book. So, while there is no promise of durability, IVR is now simply working to directly pass-through almost its entire current carry to investors.

IVR should now be seen not as a conventional income stock, but as an agency carry trade anchored in book-value. IVR works well when funding remains stable and rates are holding; it breaks otherwise. So the right question about IVR is to assess whether the current macro and funding environment can continue to support its balance sheet.

What IVR Actually Owns Today

IVR’s portfolio composition:

Everything is Agency here. There is zero non-agency exposure. 83.1% is Agency RMBS, or Residential Mortgage-Backed Securities, primarily fixed-rate pass-through securities backed by Fannie Mae, Freddie Mac, and Ginnie Mae. 15.7% is Agency CMBS, or Commercial Mortgage-Backed Securities, which are backed by commercial mortgages but still carry agency guarantees. A small 1.2% exploratory allocation is in Agency CMO, or Collateralized Mortgage Obligation, which are primarily Agency Interest-Only (IO) securities. This is a restructured pool of agency mortgages where the cash flows are sliced into different pieces (“tranches”) instead of being passed through pro-rata the way a plain agency RMBS works. The difference is not in credit risk - they are the same as with RMBS - but in cash flow behavior. In simple terms, this means some tranches get paid earlier and others later depending on how fast borrowers refinance or repay their mortgages, making these securities much more sensitive to interest-rate moves and volatility than plain RMBS.

What we don’t see here is non-agency RMBS and CMBS exposure, which used to be present earlier. We also do not see any credit-risk bets in the portfolio. So portfolio returns do not depend on borrower defaults, housing credit trends, or loss quanta. Principal is protected against credit losses through agency guarantees, so rate mechanics and not credit underwriting do the legwork so far as returns are concerned.

Credit-heavy mREITs derive returns through spread compression, borrower performance, and default dynamics. Since IVR’s entire portfolio is now Agency-backed, those variables no longer take precedence. What generates returns now are changes in interest rates, the shape of the yield curve, mortgage prepayment behavior, and duration and convexity of rates. All these go towards book value behavior, which is now the correct lens to approach IVR.

IVR’s assets are marked to market every day based on interest rates, mortgage spreads, expected prepayments, and interest-rate volatility.. They are also financed through short term repo borrowings. Repo lenders do not expect to share in the gains or losses of those assets. They expect to be repaid at par. So, any change in asset price does not flow to the liability side of the balance sheet.

This creates an interesting dynamic. When asset value goes up, equity or book value goes up; when asset value goes down, book value goes down directly. There is no intermediary here. Plus, the leverage magnifies the up/down effect. This is the reason traditional metrics don’t work well for mREITs. For an mREIT, it is entirely possible to generate good spread income to pay dividends, while eroding book value at the same time because the assets were repriced in the market.

This is why it is important to watch IVR’s book value behavior. IVR has recently been able to preserve book value while generating returns. As long as it can continue doing that, it can maintain leverage, and dividends will work as a pass-through of current balance-sheet carry rather than eroding prior capital. If that changes, book value will deteriorate, and that will be the first signal that IVR’s strategy is no longer working.

What Actually Funds the Payout

IVR’s yield is strong, but what actually funds that yield is an important question. Unlike equity REITs, there is no rental income dominance with IVR. Its payout capacity derives from asset yields, funding costs, and hedging outcomes.

On the asset side, there are differences in yields by security types. As the Q3 2025 disclosures say, fixed-rate Agency RMBS generated 5.5% yields, Agency CMBS had 4.62% yield, and Agency CMO produced 10.12% yield due to their sensitivity to prepayment behavior.

At the operating level, despite high funding costs of ~4.62% in 2025, IVR reported net interest income of roughly $54 million over the first nine months of 2025. This reflects a positive net interest spread earned across its agency mortgage portfolio after repo financing and hedging expenses, not gross asset yield.

Earnings also came from active portfolio repositioning and consequent realized gains. Over the nine months till September 30 2025, IVR posted ~$126 million in gains on its mortgage investments, largely offset by ~$117 million in derivative losses. So there was a modest net positive contribution from valuation and hedging activity - around $9 million - alongside the $54 million of net interest income. This tells us IVR pays its dividend from multiple sources, and at certain points in time, one source may offset losses in another.

Another important aspect of dividend sustainability is IVR’s large $423 million liquidity consisting of unrestricted cash of ~$180 million and unencumbered assets. This provides the fund with the flexibility to meet margin requirements, roll repo funding, etc without forced asset sales. This liquidity allows them to preserve dividends even during short-term funding stress.

What this discussion helps us understand is that IVR’s payout cannot be evaluated by standard payout ratio metrics because the dividend does not come from only a stable stream of contractual income. It also does not come from credit spread compression. It is supported by a combination of spread income, convexity-driven gains, as well as liquidity.

We can also see why the dividend can be variable. So when prepayments come in time, there is less volatility, and funding markets are fine, the portfolio can generate sufficient cash flow to sustain a high payout. When those things don’t go well, investors will feel dividend pressure. This is exactly how the pass-through model works - it passes both gains and pains through.

Leverage, Liquidity, and Regime Risk

Leverage plays a big role at IVR, since it is a near-pure agency mortgage REIT with repo. IVR’s balance sheet is large relative to equity, and funding comes primarily from short-term repo arrangements that typically reset over weeks rather than months. This makes IVR very sensitive to funding conditions.

Repo borrowings have a standard feature - they are secured against mortgage collateral and are subject to low single-digit haircuts. That means, even if there’s a small decline in asset prices, IVR needs to post additional collateral. This makes the flexibility of the balance-sheet highly dependent on asset valuation, and when prices fall, liquidity is required to stabilize leverage.

Another feature of repo borrowing is that repo maturities come in days to a few months, not years, so funding must be rolled frequently. The risk is not credit - which is agency backed - but it is a liquidity and volatility risk at some points in time. When markets are stable, IVR can finance assets efficiently; at other times, IVR becomes sensitive to margin calls and funding terms.

IVR aims to manage this exposure using a hedging strategy that tries to limit large duration mismatches. This mismatch occurs because IVR’s assets have flexible durations - this is called negative convexity - and their durations shorten/lengthen when rates fall/rise. However, IVR’s liabilities - repo borrowings - have fixed, short durations. A large duration mismatch occurs when assets suddenly behave like long-duration bonds, liabilities remain short, and equity is forced to absorb the shock. That mismatch makes the balance sheet extremely sensitive to parallel shifts in rates.

So, to manage this mismatch, IVR uses a hedging program that involves interest rate swaps and other derivatives. This keeps the effective duration gap narrow, reducing sensitivity to rate shifts across assets and liabilities. Investors need to remember, however, that hedging does not completely eliminate risks - it simply redistributes it to other instruments. So, when markets move suddenly, hedging performance falters, and can even offset asset gains.

This is where liquidity comes in. IVR has a sizable pool of cash, and this is used to support the fund when other instruments fail. This liquidity works as a buffer between short-term price shocks and permanent book value losses through forced asset sales.

Risks and Failure Modes

Several risks exist with IVR despite its recent stabilization. These are not credit risks, but structural ones tied to its nature as a balance-sheet driven levered fund whose performance is sensitive to changing market conditions.

The first risk I should mention is interest-rate volatility. I have mentioned the IVR portfolio’s negative convexity feature earlier. IVR uses derivatives to manage this, however, it is an imperfect strategy. Especially during abrupt market changes, hedging may falter, and resultant losses may even overwhelm the entire portfolio returns.

Another risk is with funding and liquidity. IVR’s reliance on short-term repo financing means borrowed funds must be rolled frequently. Funding may well be supported by agency collaterals, however, during stressed times, repo terms like haircuts and margin requirements may tighten. If asset prices fall at the same time, IVR may need cash to meet margin calls or reduce leverage. They do maintain a solid amount of cash; however, prolonged stress can hurt.

Because IVR’s dividends are not backed by contractual, long term, stable cash flows, there can be variance. IVR operates as a levered agency carry trade, so dividend will fluctuate with funding costs and other assorted reasons I mentioned earlier. This is not an anomaly.

IVR actively uses ATM equity issuance as a capital management strategy. This can improve leverage and liquidity, however it must be executed at or near book value. Otherwise, ATM issuances can dilute shareholder value.

Finally, the fund’s ultimate failure signal remains book value erosion. IVR’s assets are marked to market, and funding also comes from short term debt, so sustained declines in book value will occur before dividend cuts and forced deleveraging. This is the early warning investors must look for, and not just focus on yield cuts and reported income drawdowns.

Positioning and peer comparison

IVR is more volatile and market-sensitive than many other mREITs. It has a smaller equity base than larger agency-focused peers like AGNC Investment Corp. (AGNC) or Two Harbors Investment Corp. (TWO). That, plus less balance-sheet flexibility, makes IVR more sensitive to funding conditions and rate volatility. In past years, this has been seen in poorer price performance compared to peers despite offering a higher yield.

IVR sits in a different position from hybrid or credit-leaning mREITs like PennyMac Mortgage Investment Trust (PMT) or KKR Real Estate Finance Trust Inc. (KREF). Both these peers have more credit exposure, and cash flow is also longer duration. So income is smoother, but they come with other risks from borrower performance and property fundamentals IVR may not have. This is the trade-off you get - IVR has gotten out of credit risk altogether, but its returns are now tied to rates, volatility, and funding spreads.

So IVR should not be positioned in terms of risk, but as a purer agency carry trade vehicle with high yield. That yield will remain strong in stable regimes, however, in stressed periods, IVR can underperform relative to peers, both large and small.

Conclusion

IVR has transitioned from a difficult credit mREIT to an agency-focused one. This has caused a major shift in the economics of the fund. The recent high dividend coupled with book value stabilization indicates that the fund’s strategy is working. However, the setup is dependent on rate and market conditions. There is no durable cash flow, so return is conditional, and the whole thing gets magnified - for both good and bad - by leverage.

Given all that, IVR is best considered a Hold, not a buy-and-forget income investment. If you already own IVR, the current payout looks supportable while book value remains stable and markets remain functional. New investors should look for opportunistic entry points, with the understanding that high yield alone is not enough. If there is a sustained erosion in book value, the yield cannot be guaranteed. Income investors looking for stable and hassle-free income will find better alternatives to IVR.

data.title: Technical Levels For Major FX Pairs Ahead Of The FOMC Rate Decision
data.description:
The January FOMC is one session away, and similarly to the September Meeting, Forex Market action is quite volatile ahead of the event.

Traders are still reflecting on the many themes ongoing in Markets, including Trump Administration chaos, generational runs in Metals, Q4 Earnings, Iran, and global trade deals that are getting all over the place.

This week began on a significant gap down in the US Dollar, with the past week's steep selling flows extending to test a significant 2025 Support (on the Dollar Index).

Over the past week, post-Greenland threat selling flows have gathered such traction ahead of the FOMC that no mean reversion can take place.

Look for reactions around the 97.00 level after the meeting: a close above should signal further upside, and vice versa.

Discover further details for the Dollar Index through our latest in-depth analysis.

Some factors influencing the Dollar include the anticipated announcement of the next Fed Chair. In the meantime, there aren't many reasons except for Dollar bulls to push their bids ahead of the meeting (14:00 E.T. tomorrow).

Other FX currencies are also doing their own thing, with picture-changing releases for Antipodean currencies, the Swissie reaching its second-highest level ever against the USD, FX intervention fears in Japan, and more.

We will dive into an intraday chart outlook for all Major FX Currency pairs and provide trading levels for the upcoming huge FOMC event.

data.title: Roche Is The 'Value Play' Of The 2026 Obesity Gold Rush
data.description:
My last look at Roche Holding AG (RHHBY) followed its acquisition of 89bio for its drug targeting MASH and severe hypertriglyceridemia, pegozafermin.

The deal strengthened its cardiometabolic portfolio and followed Roche's acquisition of Carmot Therapeutics for its obesity clinical candidates, including CT-388.

I thought that Roche was slyly positioning itself as a future leader in the cardiometabolic market "without having to be face-to-face with obesity leaders like Novo Nordisk (NVO) and Eli Lilly (LLY)."

So I recommended a "Buy" rating. Roche's stock has since climbed 36%, outperforming the S&P 500's (SPY) 5% gains.

On Tuesday, Genentech (a member of the Roche Group) reported Phase 2 data for its CT-388 obesity candidate.

Below, I take a closer look at it.

Recent Developments

CT-388 is a once-weekly subcutaneous peptide that agonizes GLP-1 and GIP receptors. These are the same core receptor targets as ZEPBOUND, but 388 is supposedly differentiated by its signaling-bias/low β-arrestin recruitment design goal. This is believed to help reduce receptor internalization/desensitization (supporting more sustained pharmacology activity).

The Phase 2 obesity trial was randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled. Genentech disclosed top-line results primarily for the 24 mg arm. Placebo-adjusted weight loss after 48 weeks was 22.5% (efficacy estimand), with the company stating that there was no weight loss plateau at 48 weeks. Placebo-adjusted weight loss in the treatment-regimen estimand was 18.3%. This is a more "real-world" effectiveness under typical discontinuation/rescue patterns.

The discontinuation rate due to adverse events was 5.9% across drug arms versus 1.3% in the placebo. Moving forward, Roche plans to begin a Phase 3 obesity program in the first quarter of 2026. And the company is also evaluating 388 in obesity/overweight patients with type 2 diabetes.

So the profile looks pretty close to Zepbound. Now, a more relevant comparison would likely be next-generation obesity assets that are in similar stages of development. Eli Lilly's retatrutide (a triple agonist) is a good reference.

At 48 weeks, retatrutide 12 mg showed a placebo-adjusted weight loss of 22.1%. 388's apparent "best-case" and "more conservative reads" are separated by 4.2 percentage points in the top line. Retatrutide adverse event discontinuation was up to 16%. But this isn't a direct comparison because Roche provides only aggregate discontinuation and qualitative GI statements. So there are no dose-level AE tables and timing yet. This makes it difficult to assess whether or not 388 appears better tolerated.

Overall, I think the data are directionally supportive of a potential blockbuster therapy in obesity, with the typical caveats attached, like cross-trial comparisons and selective data reveals.

388 would join a portfolio of Roche blockbusters like OCREVUS, HEMLIBRA, VABYSMO, and TECENTRIQ.

Top Products 2024 sales (CHF bn) % of top-4 sum
Ocrevus 6.7 35.90%
Hemlibra 4.5 24.00%
Vabysmo 3.9 20.60%
Tecentriq 3.6 19.40%

Roche's obesity/cardiometabolic pipeline also includes CT-996 (oral GLP-1 receptor agonist) and petrelintide (amylin analog).

Outside of obesity, itovebi (breast cancer), columvi (DLBCL), and FIXa x FX (hemophilia) are in late-stage programs.

Of course, this is just a surface-level review of Roche's pipeline, which includes dozens of assets.

Valuation Assumptions

I conducted a discounted cash flow analysis of Roche.

Year 1 revenue of $81.79 billion is the consensus analyst estimate for 2026. My assumptions for revenue and free cash flow margin are 5% and 23%, respectively. The 23% free cash flow margin is Roche's average over the past two years.

Now, Roche's free cash flow margin has been impacted by a flurry of acquisitions. The company, in certain periods, has been able to post free cash flow margins over 30%. So I do think there's a lot of room for improvement there.

Regarding growth, as is typical for large pharma, the onus is on its ability to overcome any patent cliffs with innovation. Fortunately for Roche, I don't see any major future patent cliffs outside of mid-sized products like XOLAIR and PERJETA.

Product 2024 sales (CHF bn) LOE timing (high level) Why it matters
Xolair 2.47 Biosimilars anticipated 2026 Mid-sized erosion risk; monitor US/EU uptake and pricing
Perjeta 3.616 Biosimilars anticipated 2026 Larger exposure; pace depends on the combination of share durability and biosimilar penetration

My sensitivity analysis below shows how small changes in either growth or free cash flow margin impact Roche's intrinsic valuation.

It looks like Roche is almost equally sensitive to growth as it is to margin. I do see some opportunity for upside in both growth and margin.

For instance, if a drug like 388 can achieve $5 billion in peak annual revenue, this could add between 2 and 4 percentage points (on average, throughout the projection period) in revenue growth.

Peak CT-388 revenue % of Roche FY2026E revenue ($81.79B) What it implies
$3B 3.70% Incremental growth lever, modest
$5B 6.10% Meaningful multi-year growth boost
$8B 9.80% Near-best-in-class uptake needed

According to the sensitivity analysis, this would see Roche's intrinsic value increasing by nearly 30%.

This impact would also likely extend into its terminal value assumptions (2% → 2.5%).

Finally, my Monte Carlo simulation below runs 10,000 iterations of my DCF model.

The 5th percentile of $42.83 per share and the 95th percentile of $89.24 per share give us an idea of the "floor" and "ceiling" of the stock price, respectively, barring any major changes to valuation assumptions.

Final Words

I think what's most interesting in Roche's 388 obesity update is the 4.2-point estimand gap. This could move either way in a Phase 3 program. If the gap narrows with optimized titration and better AE management, it could become a large differentiator versus peers. But if it widens, the headline efficacy becomes much less impressive.

From a valuation perspective, the stock still screens conservatively valued under reasonable growth and free cash flow assumptions. And I suspect that if Roche can sustain mid single-digit growth while improving cash conversion, it may see upside in the long run regardless of 388 outcomes.

So I remain bullish on Roche and its ability to turn its expanding pipeline into cash-generative growth (maintain "Buy").

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Palantir: Post-Earnings Winning Habit
data.description:
Reasons to continue buying PLTR

There are numerous reasons to continue buying Palantir (PLTR)(PLTR:CA) even though my previous bullish call did not age quite well, the stock is down by 4% since then. Moreover, there were several positive earnings revisions over the last several weeks, which means that long-term forward multiples compressed notably due to stagnating share prices over the same period. Palantir's bullish earnings revisions setup is particularly important given that the company is set to release its Q4 report next week, February 2.

As the above picture shows, over the last three months, EPS forecasts increased by double-digit percentages across the board for several next quarters. Given Palantir's nearly flawless earnings surprise record, I do not think that these new EPS growth targets have become too aggressive. Moreover, history suggests that PLTR's earnings frequently become extremely powerful catalysts that generously reward analysts.

I have analyzed PLTR's share price performance one week after each quarterly earnings release over the last two years, and the setup looks extremely bullish for investors who buy PLTR before earnings. PLTR delivered massive post-earnings rallies in five out of the last eight quarterly earnings releases. The ~63% success rate looks particularly strong given that the remaining three quarters of my sample were with quite mild post-earnings selloffs. As a result, the average post-earnings share price spike over the last two years amounted to 21%. Therefore, Palantir's investors buying before earnings are frequently rewarded.

Some readers might say that the post-Q3 earnings share price movement was negative, which might create adverse momentum into the Q4 earnings season. It might be true, but it is vital to take into account the broader market's sentiment. CNN's Fear and Greed index is a useful tool to assess how the market's sentiment changed historically, and we can see that investors were under "extreme fear" in early November when PLTR released its Q3 earnings. This time the situation is different, as the market is currently moderately greedy. The "Fear and Greed" chart also shows that the setup was approximately the same back in early August when PLTR released its Q2 earnings, which was marked with a 20%+ rally during the week after the report went live. With that being said, robust market sentiment is likely to be a solid share price tailwind this time.

As I mentioned earlier, PLTR's earnings surprise record is strong. Over the last eight quarters, the average EPS surprise was approximately 12%, and the average topline surprise was ~4%. Median surprises are quite close to average numbers, which adds reliability to my approach as the distribution is not heavily skewed by outliers. Wall Street analysts forecast PLTR's Q4 revenue to be $1.34 billion (62% YoY increase), and the normalized EPS to be $0.23 (64% YoY increase). If PLTR's EPS and revenue surprises for Q4 will be approximately in line with historical averages, the company can potentially surprise investors and deliver $1.4 billion in quarterly sales together with $0.26 in normalized EPS. Therefore, it is unlikely to be a one-cent EPS surprise, which decreases the probability of investors being disappointed.

For sure, any earnings release is not only about headline numbers but also about the guidance and the management's comments during the earnings call. To understand what the guidance might be, we need to look at qualitative trends and factors. Palantir is a large beneficiary of the AI software revolution, meaning that we need to look at what surveys of C-level executives of businesses say about their AI spending plans for 2026. BCG is one of the largest consulting companies in the world, and I consider it highly reputable and reliable. According to the fresh BCG AI Radar update, investments in AI software and applications are projected to double in 2026, and business leaders are eager to invest even if these investments do not pay off rapidly. Since Palantir's Commercial segment is the company's main growth engine at the moment, such sentiment from business leaders surveyed by BCG means that the environment remains extremely positive.

Moreover, Palantir's engineers continue to actively work on expanding the number of use cases for the company's Artificial Intelligence Platform (AIP). I think so because Palantir recently expanded its strategic partnership with a South Korean giant, HD Hyundai. According to the official release, the expanded partnership will unlock various use cases for AIP such as electric systems, robotics, and marine aftermarket services. All these areas are quite large markets, and collaborating with a large conglomerate like HD Hyundai certainly adds more validation to the company's AIP. Thus, the momentum of discovering new use cases for Palantir's offerings remains strong, and it is likely to positively contribute to the FY2026 guidance.

Finally, it appears that investors are extremely cautious about Palantir's upcoming earnings release as the 14-day RSI is just above the oversold territory. I believe that is a favorable setup, as it will be much easier for Palantir to impress investors when they are cautious rather than if they were overexcited. Therefore, the technical analysis setup also looks favorable before the upcoming earnings release.

Reasons to avoid PLTR

Palantir's fundamentals remain intact, and the stock has a particularly good habit of soaring post-earnings. However, there is an apparent elephant in the room, which is Palantir's extremely growth-oriented valuation. Just look at these multiples; they are simply sky-high. Such valuation means that investors expect nearly flawless execution over the next several years, which will allow PLTR to deliver exceptional EPS growth.

The combination of facts like businesses planning to double down on their AI spending and Palantir rapidly expanding the number of use cases for AIP will certainly result in a massive P/E ratio compression. However, the biggest problem is that it is impossible to quantify reliably Palantir's long-term EPS growth trajectory to understand what its P/E ratio will look like closer to 2030. Of course, C-level executives of businesses say that they are ready to increase AI investments even if they don't see the positive effect in 2026, but we cannot be confident that they will remain that patient in 2028 or 2029 as well. Therefore, whether Palantir's valuation is fair or not is a highly controversial question.

Moreover, there are too many big uncertainties beyond the AI industry itself. We currently observe a highly complicated geopolitical landscape where international relationships of the U.S. are getting really stress-tested. In case the U.S.'s historically strong political bonds and Europe become weaker, it might adversely affect Palantir's potential to drive international expansion. Thus, the company's revenue growth pace might deteriorate significantly once Palantir's domestic penetration achieves peak levels.

The verdict

Palantir's good habit of demonstrating massive rallies post-earnings is the main reason why I am adding more shares to my portfolio. Both fundamental and technical setups look favorable for PLTR to deliver large earnings and guidance surprises. Palantir's sky-high multiples make investing large amounts quite scary, meaning that I reiterate a plain buy for PLTR.

data.title: IEF: Staying Bullish, Despite The Noise
data.description:
This article provide an updated outlook on the iShares 7-10 Year Treasury Bond ETF (NASDAQ:IEF), which I covered on numerous occasions last year. It's an unusual time for U.S. Treasuries, where market psychology and narrative seem to dominate analysis. This provides a perfect opportunity to identify value, which is exactly what I aimed to do with this analysis.

Last time around, I communicated a positive outlook on IEF, citing its lucrative risk premium. Upon reviewal, I have decided to stick with my outlook; here's why.

IEF's Recent Performance and Portfolio Review

The iShares 7-10 Year Treasury Bond ETF has delivered 8.06% in total return over the past year, largely driven by downward levelling of the U.S. Treasury yield curve and elevated distribution yields. The vehicle maintains an expense ratio of 0.15%, a monthly income-based distribution structure, and possesses a 30-day SEC yield of 3.97%. In addition, IEF ETF's current weighted average maturity stands at 8.46 years, effective duration 6.96 years, and convexity 58 basis points, illustrating exposure to the belly of the curve with mild secondary-affect gain prospects. In terms of its total risk premium, IEF has a weighted average yield-to-maturity of 4.14%.

Statistical Analysis

I tested a butterfly spread this morning (long 2-year yield, long 20-year yield, short 10-year yield), resembling equal long positions in the 2 and 10-year yield, and a short position in the 10-year yield. The butterfly isn't a trade set-up, as it ignores duration-adjusted position sizing and convexity biases. Instead, it provides a sense of which parts of the curve are statistically rich or cheap. Based on my test, the butterfly has a spread of negative 3.1%, suggesting the belly of the curve (10Y) provides a superior premium to a combination of the 2-year and 20-year.

I took the analysis a step further by observing z-scores. First. I'd like to cite the 10-2 spread widening to ~64 basis points on January 23rd, up from 36 basis points the same time last year and the 50 basis points witnessed 6-months ago. I smoothed the spread by running a 52-week rolling spread and subsequently transformed the value into a z-score. As shown by Figure 3, the 10-2 z-score is 1.01 sigma rich and the 20-10 z-score is around 0.377 sigma rich (sigma poor from the 10-year yield's perspective). The illustration suggests statistical relative value is present in the 10-year yield, especially relative to the front-end – a macro discussion is conveyed, later in the article, to support the claim.

Furthermore, I tested the half-lives of each spread and embedded my findings in figure 5. The blank spots on the line chart reveals periods where the drift term was undetected. In simple terms, it reveals periods where mean-reversion wasn't in play. That set aside, mean-reversion has shown consistent representation with the half life for the 10-2 being 6-weeks and the half life for the 20-10 being 2 weeks (according to my tests).

Lastly, I present figure 5, which isolated spread volatility. The spread volatility is important to note, as highly volatile spreads signal noise, whereas stable volatility, provides cleaner mean-reversion opportunities. Per my test, the spread volatility cooled throughout last year, largely due to the MOVE Index cooling. Secondarily, mean-reversion has already occurred (shown by the z-stats); in my experience, with a polynomial distribution, the volatility of mean-reversion reduces nearer to the moving average.

The conclusions I've made from the tests are:

  • The 10-year yield statistical relative value versus the 2-year yield, relative to periods post-2020.
  • The 20-year shows statistical value versus the 10, but now enough to suggest a dislocation (I deem 0.377 sigma is quite small).
  • Combined, the belly of the curve seems highly appealing, at this stage. The longer-end might be appealing to some, but I'd personally like seeing a more substantial premium before engaging.

Macro & Market Microstructure

Disinflation and a Lower Inflation Premium

I believe the U.S.' disinflation trend has consolidated. Before I discuss my outlook, I'd like to share external data. As illustrated by figure 6, the Cleveland Federal Reserve's nowcasting deems CPI at 2.35% for January. If realised, CPI would show a clear downtrend versus December's 2.7% and October's 3%.

The inflation term structure has shown a dramatic drop at the short-end, against the backdrop of CPI settling at-or-below estimates in recent months. For example, the one-year horizon has shifted down by 60 basis points month-over-month to 2.6%, back to pre-liberation day levels. Concurrently, the inflation premium has consolidated at 40 basis points, since August last year, suggesting inflation re-anchoring. The breakeven rate has ticked up to 232 basis points, after retreating to 220 basis point level, but I think this might be due to the bond market's over-reaction to monetary policy; we'll likely see breakeven come down if January CPI settles in-line.

The final set of 'direct data' I'd like to point out, is the Fed futures market, which expects one-to-two target rate cuts for the year. I wouldn't rely on the futures market too much at this stage, seeing as speculation of a new Federal Reserve Chair and a renewed wave of tariff threats have sent markets into a seemingly speculative environment.

Now that we've had a look at directly related to inflation, I'd like to share what I think about inflation and IEF's prospects leading forward.

I believe CPI will settle slightly below the market's estimate, in the low 2% environment (PCE might settle even lower, due to its substitution-effect). My base case stems from the fact that the unemployment rate of 4.4% has exceeded the natural rate of unemployment (~4.3%), consumer confidence remains subdued, and that inflation is non-monotonic, in other words, it reverts to mean. A counter-argument might be the output-gap, which has continued to surge, reaching 2.2% of GDP in Q3, 2025, likely due to productivity gains from AI and expansionary monetary policy. That being said, I'd like to think that the labour market will translate into a softer output gap in 2026 – I'd also highlight the fact that the output gains via AI, has partly been linked to capital investment, rather than a pure change in the production function – investment might show signs of slowing.

If realised, a lower inflation rate would likely drop the yield curve's level, giving IEF price support via lower duration. Of course, lower coupons will emerge in due course. However, the effect would likely be lagged, as IEF would need to reshuffle its asset base by a considerable amount before its lower coupon base would cancel out price gains. Some might argue that steepening will occur if additional rate cuts emerge – I'd highlight three things: Firstly, the U.S. is already 175 basis points in rate-cuts deep, secondly, the curve has steepened by a tremendous amount last year (we might see some levelling due to basic interest rates and curve dynamics), and third, we could see post-2026 tightening being priced into the curve at some stage this year (on the basis of potential re-inflation post 2026/27).

Herewith is a diagram illustrating the yield curve and its movement over the last twelve months. I'll use it as a reference for points made, later in the analysis.

Productivity Growth Expectations Might Cool

The U.S. yield curve has remained high for a few years. Although inflation and a fiscal risk premium can be attributed to the curve's level (especially at the longer end), I think many have forgotten that productivity growth also boosts yields. In Citigroup's (C) view and my own, productivity has gained due to the pandemic, due to higher total factor productivity (technology) and growth in capital investment. However, with government debt levels running high and marginal gains of tech-based productivity growth likely to diminish in-time, I won't be surprised seeing productivity gains slow in the near future, leading to lower mid-to-longer term yields.

Fiscal Risk Premium Consolidation

The U.S. fiscal risk premium is a big talking point, given the shift in political delivery from Washington. I think the uncertainty of the fiscal risk premium is probably the primary reason why I still prefer the belly of the curve, as opposed to the very-longer end. The market has somewhat normalised Washington's stance and USD-debasement has likely being price-in to a great extent. I believe investors might consolidating the fiscal risk premium at the 10-year, but remain coy about its very-long term influence.

Read more about the fiscal risk premium, via this link.

Lower Volatility and A Temporary Rise In Demand For Immediacy

Treasury volatility cooled significantly in 2025, allowing IEF to deliver a 1-year total return of 8.06% and the U.S. 10-year yield to drop by 43 basis points year-over-year. We might see continuation in this trend, given that inflation is re-anchoring. That being said, U.S. 10-year yields have spiked by 21 basis points in the past three months, on the basis of Fed Chair speculation and U.S. allies developing distrust in their relationship with Washington. In my opinion, this relationship forms an excellent buying opportunity, where a lower volatility regime meets a temporary rise in the demand for immediacy (investors can buy in a lower vol regime, while providing immediate liquidity to panic sellers and adversaries).

The 60/40 Portfolio's Risk Premia

AQR Capital Management was recently quoted saying: "The expected real return of a global 60/40 portfolio is 3.4%, around 1.5% higher than the all-time low reached in 2021, but still well below the long-term U.S. average of nearly 5% since 1900."

Why did I mention the above? Well, it shows that normalisation might be occurring, whereby the premia available via the 60/40 is starting to make sense again. In my view, it might be a good idea opting for Treasuries at this stage, given a return of demand for immediacy. In tandem, I'm in favour of maintaining equity exposure, but would use this period to hoard bonds, while waiting for demand for immediacy to return to the stock market, before I buy again.

Important Side Note: IEF is a duration-targeted ETF, whereby it opts to maintain a duration between 7-10 years. The ETFs premia will differ greatly from an investor who keeps a held-to-maturity bond. In fact, there's a fair argument that ETFs such as IEF ETF are to be treated as cyclical instruments, as opposed to long-term investment vehicles.

Risk Factors

Supply-Growth

The U.S. debt supply has grown substantially since the pro-COVID great financial liberation era, and even more-so after the pandemic. I think we could see supply being constrained soon, on the basis of bond holders casting doubt over interest costs, fiscal dominance, and the U.S.' credibility as a ballast. That being said, Washington seems quite unhinged about fiscal deficits at this stage, shifting focus to trade flows. A continued increase in the supply of debt, could drag 7-10-year yields up even further.

As a trade-off to the above, the U.S. Federal Reserve has stopped its quantitative tightening program. We might see balance sheet expansion in the coming years, including acquisitions of longer-term US. Treasuries. However, this in itself, doesn't guarantee higher net demand for U.S. Treasuries.

Ongoing Fiscal Risk

This sub-section somewhat relates to what I mentioned in the previous section. The "sell the U.S. trade" remains a material threat. Obviously, we'll need to see the narrative translate into action, which could be constrained due to two reasons: 1) Fed markets have the depth of U.S. markets, making full repatriation difficult, 2) Excess selling (by foreign holders) would probably result in losses for bond holders, which would not sit well with pension fund and insurance regulators. Nonetheless, a gradual shift out of U.S.-conviction portfolios (by Sovereign Wealth Funds, Governments, and Pension funds) could take shape.

FX Risk

The USD-debasement narrative has added headwinds to the USD and might translate into headwinds for U.S. Treasuries. The reliance on gold reserves and a shift to less USD-centric FX portfolios is well underway. Moreover, the U.S. has been in an expansionary monetary cycle, for the past two years. In addition, carry on emerging market FX has improved, and therefore lower demand for USD has occurred via that angle. The USD Index (DXY) currently trades ~2.81 standard deviations below its 12-month moving average. Lower appetite for the USD might translate into lower appetite for 7-10 year U.S. Treasuries and IEF ETF alike.

The Carry-Trade (Japan)

The U.S.-Japan carry trade, which includes purchases of U.S. Treasuries via funding from Japan has faced pressure lately, after Japan exited its negative rate regime. According to Desmond Lachman, the size of the carry trade is around $500 billion in notional, depending on time measured and methodology. Other sources, such as the Financial times have suggested size is usually half of that at $250 billion - excluding off-balance sheet items. Unwinding of the strategy could severely impact markets. That being said, I believe investors will migrate to other methods of funding. For example, we might see more investors fund through short-term repo markets. Sure, at lower profitability, but pressure to generate alpha remains, and portability needs to be considered.

IEF Can Suffer from left-tail risk

IEF has suffered from double-digit max drawdowns, even before the use case of U.S. Treasuries as safe-haven assets had been called into question. The ETF also possesses a 5% monthly value-at-risk of 2.82%, which I deem noteworthy, for a sovereign bond vehicle. In other words, isolation against market-based tail risk isn't guaranteed.

Conclusion

I'm re-iterating my bullish outlook on IEF ETF, which I'll likely sustain until its z-score relative to other parts of the curve settles down or if 10-year yields revert to the 400 basis point level. I acknowledge risks such as the USD debasement trade, the Japan carry trade's fragility, and fiscal dominance. However, I also believe the market has priced such factors into the yield already and that capital repatriation might face obstacles. I expect moderate cooling in 10-year yields this year and believe IEF ETF provides a solid total return opportunity.

data.title: Harmony Biosciences: Still Undervalued But Unappealing Risk/Reward (Downgrade)
data.description:
Article Thesis

I wrote about Harmony Biosciences (HRMY) in March last year and assigned it a “Buy” rating. My strategy for small biotech companies is to go with small positions (between ¼ and ½ initial position) for about 10 names, due to very high risks associated, especially with single-product companies. In this way, even if one or two or of them crashed, or even if one of them went bankrupt, my portfolio would not be much affected.

I generally go with ~¼ position for single-product companies and with ~1/2 for more diversified ones. For Harmony, I went with ~0.33x position, with one blockbuster product, and several others in their pipeline.

However, important things happened in the meantime. One of their pipeline candidates failed. Pressure on US drug prices intensified, and Harmony just holds a license from Bioprojet to manufacture and commercialize Wakix specifically in the US.

Therefore, a reassessment of their business, fair value, short-term outlook is in order, but also in the context of a broader biotech portfolio.

Business Analysis

Like mentioned in the introduction, Harmony holds the license to commercialize Wakix (pitolisant) in the US for narcolepsy, and this is their only commercial product. Wakix is indeed a superior product, being the only non-scheduled treatment for daytime sleepiness, with the company confident that this is an opportunity for “$1B+ in narcolepsy alone”. Besides, the company is trying to expand its market with two new formulas: Pitolisant GR (gastro-resistant) and Pitolisant HD (High Dose):

There are, however, some important caveats, besides the pressure on US drug prices mentioned in the introduction:

  • Pitolisant GR will obviously have a cannibalization effect, mostly taking market from Wakix, but with the potential to increase the number of patients, indeed.
  • A generic version of Wakix is estimated for 2031. Of course, Pitolisant GR’s main purpose is to extend the Pitolisant franchise to the mid-2040s, but it’s likely that a generic version of the initial Pitolisant will take market share from 2031.
  • Pitolisant HD’s main purpose is to expand the Pitolisant patient base into new indications, like IH (idiopathic hypersomnia). However, FDA refused last year their application of Pitolisant for IH, and it’s unclear if the new dosage can make the difference.

Then, let’s take a look at their pipeline:

With orphan drug designation status, I see Pitolisant for PWS (Prader Willi Syndrome) with more chances than the shaky Pitolisant HD for IH.

But the most important to notice is that ZYN-002, which was in March a candidate in an advanced stage for FXS (Fragile X Syndrome) and 22q (Deletion Syndrome), is withdrawn from their pipeline for both indications after its failure for FXS.

And we have EPX-100 as the more important candidate in their pipeline, with its two indications. While I do see more chances than for ZYN-002, there is a similar risk: a potential failure for one indication could trigger discontinuation for both.

Fair Value

With about $868M revenue for 2025 and about 8500 Wakix patients, that suggests ~$100K annual costs for Wakix, which, in my non-professional opinion, is a high cost:

My future revenue estimates are based on the following assumptions:

  • Pitolisant: the number of patients reaching 15K in 2030, but annual price decreasing to $40K due to US drug price pressure but also due to the launch of Pitolisant GR and HD, for $600M sales; then, decreasing from $300M in 2031 (generic launch) to $100M in 2034.
  • Pitolisant GR: 5K patients in 2030 (on top of the 15K for Pitolisant) x $50K annual price = $250M revenue; then, relatively constant until 2034 due to a Pitolisant generic launch.
  • Pitolisant PWS: launch in 2027 for a market of $600M and growing. However, with just a part if the market for Harmony related to excessive daytime sleepiness and behavioral symptoms, and with ~75% success probability, I model $100M peak sales in 2034.
  • Pitolisant HD: with ~$0.45B US estimated market for IH in 2033, but a low probability of success after the initial failure of Pitolisant, and ~$0.6B US estimated market for DM1, I model $150M peak sales: $50M for narcolepsy, $50M for IH, $50M for DM1.
  • EPX-100 for DS (Dravet Syndrome) and LGS (Lenox-Gastaut Syndrome): the company sees $1B-$2B potential peak sales for each of the two, but with potential payments to Epygenix of up to $130 million based on development and regulatory milestones, and up to $515 million if certain sales milestones are achieved, with other new drugs under development and with some probability factor, I model only ~$150M revenue for each in 2034.
